description_long: This object is a sculpted stone guard stone in the form of a kneeling ancient Egyptian figure, likely a pharaoh, with a traditional nemes headdress and a false beard. The figure is depicted with both arms resting on its knees, holding two spherical objects in its hands. The stone surface is light beige with subtle variations in tone, and the carving is detailed, especially around the facial features, headdress, and musculature. The back of the figure shows the pleated texture of the headdress and a solid, block-like support extending from the base to the upper back. The base is roughly rectangular and appears robust, designed to anchor the figure securely. The overall impression is of a monumental, durable object intended to serve both a protective and decorative function in an urban or ceremonial setting.
description: A large, intricately carved stone guard stone depicting a kneeling ancient Egyptian figure with a headdress, holding two spheres, and set on a sturdy rectangular base.
description_view_0: Side view showing the left profile of the kneeling figure, highlighting the facial features, arm position, and the two spheres held in its hands.
description_view_1: Rear view displaying the pleated headdress, broad shoulders, and the solid support structure running down the back.
description_view_2: Side view from the right, mirroring the left profile but with clearer visibility of the right arm and the rectangular base.
description_view_3: Frontal view emphasizing the symmetrical pose, facial details, headdress, and the two spheres held forward on the knees.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to historical sites, museums, or as a decorative and protective barrier at the edge of plazas, courtyards, or along pedestrian walkways in urban environments.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright on its rectangular base, possibly anchored or partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show smoothing and slight discoloration on protruding surfaces such as the knees, hands, and headdress due to weathering and occasional contact. Edges of the base may exhibit minor chipping from incidental impacts or foot traffic. Surface erosion may be present from prolonged exposure to rain and wind.
